title: Offline-First Architecture
description: Room as single source of truth, sync with WorkManager, conflict resolution, and cache invalidation

references.title: Android Developers — Build an offline-first app
Martin Kleppmann — CRDTs and the Quest for Distributed Consistency

lesson:
The Offline-First Principle

In an offline-first app, Room is the single source of truth. The network is just a sync mechanism. The UI never reads from the network directly — it always observes Room. When network data arrives, it goes into Room, and Room notifies the UI via Flow. This means the app works identically whether the device is online, offline, on a plane, or in a subway tunnel.

The Golden Rule:
Room IS the app's data. The API is just a way to keep Room up to date.

If you can only remember one thing: UI reads Room. Network writes to Room. Never skip Room.

Data Flow Architecture

┌──────────┐ observes ┌────────────┐ reads ┌──────────┐ │ │ StateFlow │ │ Flow │ │ │ UI │ ◄────────────── │ ViewModel │ ◄─────────── │ Room │ │ (Compose)│ │ │ │ (SQLite) │ └──────────┘ └─────┬──────┘ └────▲─────┘ │ │ calls│ writes│ ▼ │ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────┴──────┐ │ │ ────────► │ │ │ Repository │ upsert │ Room │ │ │ ◄──────── │ │ └──────┬───────┘ read └─────────────┘ │ sync │ ▼ ┌──────────────┐ │ REST API │ │ (Retrofit) │ └──────────────┘ Key insight: the arrow from API → UI goes THROUGH Room. There is no direct API → UI path.

Room as the Local Layer

Your Room entity needs a syncStatus field to track what's been synced and what hasn't:

// Sync status tracks each row's relationship with the server enum class SyncStatus { SYNCED, // matches server state PENDING, // created/edited locally, not yet pushed DELETING // marked for deletion, not yet confirmed by server } @Entity(tableName = "notes") data class NoteEntity( @PrimaryKey val id: String = UUID.randomUUID().toString(), val title: String, val content: String, val updatedAt: Long = System.currentTimeMillis(), val syncStatus: SyncStatus = SyncStatus.PENDING )

The DAO uses Flow return types so the UI reactively updates when data changes, and @Upsert to handle insert-or-update in one operation:

@Dao interface NoteDao { // UI observes this — automatically re-emits when data changes @Query("SELECT * FROM notes WHERE syncStatus != 'DELETING' ORDER BY updatedAt DESC") fun observeAll(): Flow<List<NoteEntity>> @Query("SELECT * FROM notes WHERE id = :id") fun observeById(id: String): Flow<NoteEntity?> // Upsert = INSERT or UPDATE if primary key exists @Upsert suspend fun upsert(note: NoteEntity) @Upsert suspend fun upsertAll(notes: List<NoteEntity>) // For sync: find everything that needs to be pushed @Query("SELECT * FROM notes WHERE syncStatus = 'PENDING'") suspend fun getPending(): List<NoteEntity> @Query("SELECT * FROM notes WHERE syncStatus = 'DELETING'") suspend fun getDeleting(): List<NoteEntity> @Query("DELETE FROM notes WHERE id = :id") suspend fun hardDelete(id: String) }

The Read Path: UI Always Observes Room

The UI never waits for a network call. It shows whatever Room has immediately, then refreshes in the background:

class NoteRepository( private val dao: NoteDao, private val api: NoteApi, private val syncScheduler: SyncScheduler ) { // READ: UI observes this Flow — Room is the source of truth fun observeNotes(): Flow<List<NoteEntity>> = dao.observeAll() // REFRESH: fetch from API, write to Room, UI auto-updates suspend fun refresh() { val remoteNotes = api.getAllNotes() // network call val entities = remoteNotes.map { it.toEntity(SyncStatus.SYNCED) } dao.upsertAll(entities) // Room notifies Flow observers } } // In ViewModel: class NoteListViewModel( private val repository: NoteRepository ) : ViewModel() { val notes: StateFlow<List<NoteEntity>> = repository .observeNotes() .stateIn(viewModelScope, SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), emptyList()) init { viewModelScope.launch { try { repository.refresh() } catch (e: Exception) { // Network failed? No problem — UI shows cached data } } } }
Why this works offline:
On first launch with no network, refresh() throws — but the UI is observing dao.observeAll(), which returns whatever's in Room (possibly empty). On second launch, Room has cached data from the last successful sync. The user sees stale-but-present data instantly, with no loading spinner.

The Write Path: Room First, Then Sync

When the user creates or edits data, write to Room immediately with PENDING status, then schedule a background sync:

class NoteRepository( private val dao: NoteDao, private val api: NoteApi, private val syncScheduler: SyncScheduler ) { // WRITE: save locally first, schedule background sync suspend fun saveNote(note: NoteEntity) { dao.upsert(note.copy(syncStatus = SyncStatus.PENDING)) syncScheduler.scheduleSync() // WorkManager enqueues a one-time sync } suspend fun deleteNote(id: String) { // Soft-delete: mark as DELETING so UI hides it, but row persists for sync dao.upsert(dao.getById(id).copy(syncStatus = SyncStatus.DELETING)) syncScheduler.scheduleSync() } } // SyncScheduler wraps WorkManager details class SyncScheduler(private val context: Context) { fun scheduleSync() { val request = OneTimeWorkRequestBuilder<SyncWorker>() .setConstraints( Constraints.Builder() .setRequiredNetworkType(NetworkType.CONNECTED) .build() ) .setBackoffCriteria( BackoffPolicy.EXPONENTIAL, WorkRequest.MIN_BACKOFF_MILLIS, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS ) .build() WorkManager.getInstance(context) .enqueueUniqueWork("note_sync", ExistingWorkPolicy.REPLACE, request) } }
Why REPLACE policy:
If the user saves three notes in quick succession, we don't need three sync jobs. REPLACE cancels the pending sync and creates a new one, which will push all three changes at once. This is a natural batching mechanism.

THE WORKER ROUTING PROBLEM

This is the most common architectural mistake in offline-first apps. Workers need to sync data — but how they access that data determines whether your architecture stays clean or becomes a tangled mess.

The Wrong Way: Worker calls DAO and API directly
If your Worker calls dao.getPending() and api.pushNote() directly, you now have two paths to your data: the Repository (used by ViewModel) and the Worker (bypassing Repository). Any business logic in Repository — validation, transformation, conflict resolution — gets duplicated or skipped.
THE WRONG WAY THE RIGHT WAY ────────────── ───────────────── ViewModel ViewModel │ │ ▼ ▼ Repository ──► Room Repository ──► Room │ ▲ │ ▲ ▼ │ ▼ │ API │ API │ │ │ SyncWorker ────┘ ◄── BAD! SyncWorker ─────┘ │ Two paths │ ▼ to data! ▼ API ◄──────────── Duplicated Repository ◄── GOOD! logic! │ One path ▼ to data! Room + API

The fix: Workers call Repository, not DAO/API directly. The Repository is the single gateway to your data, no matter who's calling:

class SyncWorker( context: Context, params: WorkerParameters, private val repository: NoteRepository // injected via HiltWorker ) : CoroutineWorker(context, params) { override suspend fun doWork(): Result { return try { repository.performSync() // Worker calls Repository, not DAO/API Result.success() } catch (e: Exception) { if (runAttemptCount < 3) Result.retry() else Result.failure() } } } // In Repository — the sync logic lives here, alongside all other data logic class NoteRepository(...) { suspend fun performSync() { // Push pending changes val pending = dao.getPending() for (note in pending) { val serverNote = api.pushNote(note.toDto()) dao.upsert(note.copy(syncStatus = SyncStatus.SYNCED, updatedAt = serverNote.updatedAt)) } // Delete items marked for deletion val deleting = dao.getDeleting() for (note in deleting) { api.deleteNote(note.id) dao.hardDelete(note.id) } // Pull latest from server refresh() } }

Pragmatic Approach: Repository Owns Scheduling

In the pragmatic approach, the Repository owns the SyncScheduler directly. When the ViewModel calls repository.saveNote(), the Repository saves to Room AND schedules the sync — the ViewModel doesn't need to know about WorkManager at all:

class NoteRepository( private val dao: NoteDao, private val api: NoteApi, private val syncScheduler: SyncScheduler // Repository owns this ) { fun observeNotes(): Flow<List<NoteEntity>> = dao.observeAll() suspend fun saveNote(note: NoteEntity) { dao.upsert(note.copy(syncStatus = SyncStatus.PENDING)) syncScheduler.scheduleSync() // Repository handles scheduling } suspend fun refresh() { /* fetch from API, upsert into Room */ } suspend fun performSync() { /* push pending, pull latest */ } } // ViewModel is clean — no knowledge of sync mechanics class NoteViewModel(private val repo: NoteRepository) : ViewModel() { fun save(title: String, content: String) { viewModelScope.launch { repo.saveNote(NoteEntity(title = title, content = content)) } } }

This is the right choice for most apps. It's simple, testable, and keeps sync details out of the ViewModel.

Clean Architecture Approach: UseCase Layer

In Clean Architecture, a UseCase (or Interactor) sits between the ViewModel and Repository. Each UseCase represents one action the user can take:

class SaveNoteUseCase( private val noteRepository: NoteRepository, private val analyticsRepository: AnalyticsRepository ) { suspend operator fun invoke(note: NoteEntity) { noteRepository.saveNote(note) analyticsRepository.trackEvent("note_saved", mapOf("id" to note.id)) } } class NoteViewModel( private val saveNote: SaveNoteUseCase, observeNotes: ObserveNotesUseCase ) : ViewModel() { val notes = observeNotes().stateIn(...) fun save(title: String, content: String) { viewModelScope.launch { saveNote(NoteEntity(title = title, content = content)) } } }

When UseCases Earn Their Keep

UseCases are worth it when they coordinate multiple repositories:
- SaveNoteUseCase → NoteRepository + AnalyticsRepository + NotificationRepository
- ArchiveProjectUseCase → ProjectRepository + TaskRepository + TeamRepository
- CheckoutCartUseCase → CartRepository + InventoryRepository + PaymentRepository

UseCases are just pass-throughs when:
- GetNotesUseCase just calls repo.observeNotes() and nothing else
- This adds a file, a class, and an injection point for zero benefit

The pragmatic rule: start without UseCases. Extract one when a ViewModel method touches 2+ repositories or when the same logic appears in 2+ ViewModels.

Network State Detection

Use ConnectivityManager.NetworkCallback wrapped in a callbackFlow to get a reactive stream of network state:

class NetworkMonitor(context: Context) { private val connectivityManager = context.getSystemService(Context.CONNECTIVITY_SERVICE) as ConnectivityManager val isOnline: Flow<Boolean> = callbackFlow { val callback = object : ConnectivityManager.NetworkCallback() { private val networks = mutableSetOf<Network>() override fun onAvailable(network: Network) { networks += network trySend(true) } override fun onLost(network: Network) { networks -= network trySend(networks.isNotEmpty()) } } val request = NetworkRequest.Builder() .addCapability(NetworkCapabilities.NET_CAPABILITY_INTERNET) .build() connectivityManager.registerNetworkCallback(request, callback) // Emit current state immediately val currentNetwork = connectivityManager.activeNetwork val caps = connectivityManager.getNetworkCapabilities(currentNetwork) trySend(caps?.hasCapability(NetworkCapabilities.NET_CAPABILITY_INTERNET) == true) awaitClose { connectivityManager.unregisterNetworkCallback(callback) } }.distinctUntilChanged() } // In ViewModel — trigger sync when back online class NoteListViewModel( private val repository: NoteRepository, networkMonitor: NetworkMonitor ) : ViewModel() { init { viewModelScope.launch { networkMonitor.isOnline .filter { it } // only when coming online .collect { try { repository.refresh() } catch (_: Exception) {} } } } }

Conflict Resolution Strategies

When the user edits a note offline and someone else edits the same note on another device, you have a conflict. There's no single right answer — each strategy has trade-offs:

StrategyHow It WorksBest ForDownside
Last-Write-WinsHighest timestamp winsSimple apps, user settingsSilent data loss
Server-WinsServer version always overwrites localRead-heavy apps, reference dataDiscards offline edits
Client-WinsLocal version always overwrites serverSingle-user appsOverwrites other devices
Manual ResolutionShow both versions, user picksCritical data (docs, medical)UX friction
Field-Level MergeMerge non-conflicting fields, flag conflictsForms with independent fieldsComplex to implement
CRDTsData structures that auto-merge without conflictsCollaborative editingComplex, limited data types
// Last-Write-Wins implementation in Repository suspend fun mergeFromServer(remote: NoteDto) { val local = dao.getById(remote.id) when { local == null -> { // New note from server — just insert dao.upsert(remote.toEntity(SyncStatus.SYNCED)) } local.syncStatus == SyncStatus.SYNCED -> { // No local changes — safe to overwrite dao.upsert(remote.toEntity(SyncStatus.SYNCED)) } remote.updatedAt > local.updatedAt -> { // Server is newer — server wins (last-write-wins) dao.upsert(remote.toEntity(SyncStatus.SYNCED)) } else -> { // Local is newer — keep local, it will be pushed on next sync } } } // Field-Level Merge — more sophisticated suspend fun fieldLevelMerge(local: NoteEntity, remote: NoteDto, base: NoteDto) { val merged = NoteEntity( id = local.id, // If only one side changed the title, use that one title = when { local.title != base.title && remote.title == base.title -> local.title local.title == base.title && remote.title != base.title -> remote.title local.title == remote.title -> local.title // same change, no conflict else -> local.title // CONFLICT: both changed — flag for user }, content = /* same logic for content */, syncStatus = SyncStatus.PENDING ) dao.upsert(merged) }

Cache Invalidation

How do you know when cached data is stale? Four strategies, from simplest to most sophisticated:

// 1. TTL (Time-To-Live) — simplest approach class NoteRepository(...) { private var lastFetchTime: Long = 0 private val TTL = 5.minutes.inWholeMilliseconds suspend fun getNotesWithTtl(): Flow<List<NoteEntity>> { if (System.currentTimeMillis() - lastFetchTime > TTL) { refresh() lastFetchTime = System.currentTimeMillis() } return dao.observeAll() } } // 2. ETag / If-Modified-Since — server tells you if data changed suspend fun refreshWithEtag() { val etag = prefs.getString("notes_etag", null) val response = api.getNotes(ifNoneMatch = etag) // If-None-Match header when (response.code()) { 200 -> { // Data changed — update cache dao.upsertAll(response.body()!!.map { it.toEntity() }) prefs.edit { putString("notes_etag", response.headers()["ETag"]) } } 304 -> { // Not Modified — cache is still valid, do nothing } } } // 3. Push notifications (FCM) — server tells client to refresh class SyncFcmService : FirebaseMessagingService() { override fun onMessageReceived(message: RemoteMessage) { if (message.data["type"] == "notes_updated") { // Schedule a sync to pull latest data SyncScheduler(this).scheduleSync() } } } // 4. Refresh-on-focus — re-fetch when app comes to foreground class MainActivity : ComponentActivity() { override fun onResume() { super.onResume() noteViewModel.refreshIfStale() } }
In practice, combine them:
- TTL to avoid hitting the network on every screen visit
- ETag to avoid re-downloading unchanged data
- FCM push for near-real-time updates of important data
- Refresh-on-focus as a safety net to catch up after long backgrounding

Full Example: Notes App with Sync

Putting it all together — a complete offline-first notes app with sync, conflict handling, and offline status:

// ── Entity ────────────────────────────────────────── @Entity(tableName = "notes") data class NoteEntity( @PrimaryKey val id: String = UUID.randomUUID().toString(), val title: String, val content: String, val updatedAt: Long = System.currentTimeMillis(), val syncStatus: SyncStatus = SyncStatus.PENDING, val conflictServerVersion: String? = null // non-null = conflict exists ) // ── DAO ───────────────────────────────────────────── @Dao interface NoteDao { @Query("SELECT * FROM notes WHERE syncStatus != 'DELETING' ORDER BY updatedAt DESC") fun observeAll(): Flow<List<NoteEntity>> @Query("SELECT * FROM notes WHERE id = :id") suspend fun getById(id: String): NoteEntity? @Upsert suspend fun upsert(note: NoteEntity) @Upsert suspend fun upsertAll(notes: List<NoteEntity>) @Query("SELECT * FROM notes WHERE syncStatus = 'PENDING'") suspend fun getPending(): List<NoteEntity> @Query("SELECT * FROM notes WHERE syncStatus = 'DELETING'") suspend fun getDeleting(): List<NoteEntity> @Query("DELETE FROM notes WHERE id = :id") suspend fun hardDelete(id: String) @Query("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM notes WHERE syncStatus != 'SYNCED'") fun observeUnsyncedCount(): Flow<Int> } // ── Repository ────────────────────────────────────── class NoteRepository( private val dao: NoteDao, private val api: NoteApi, private val syncScheduler: SyncScheduler ) { fun observeNotes(): Flow<List<NoteEntity>> = dao.observeAll() fun observeUnsyncedCount(): Flow<Int> = dao.observeUnsyncedCount() suspend fun saveNote(note: NoteEntity) { dao.upsert(note.copy( syncStatus = SyncStatus.PENDING, updatedAt = System.currentTimeMillis() )) syncScheduler.scheduleSync() } suspend fun deleteNote(id: String) { val note = dao.getById(id) ?: return dao.upsert(note.copy(syncStatus = SyncStatus.DELETING)) syncScheduler.scheduleSync() } suspend fun refresh() { val remoteNotes = api.getAllNotes() for (remote in remoteNotes) { mergeFromServer(remote) } } suspend fun performSync() { // 1. Push pending creates/updates for (note in dao.getPending()) { try { val response = api.pushNote(note.toDto()) if (response.isSuccessful) { dao.upsert(note.copy(syncStatus = SyncStatus.SYNCED)) } else if (response.code() == 409) { // Conflict! Store server version for manual resolution val serverVersion = api.getNote(note.id) dao.upsert(note.copy( conflictServerVersion = Json.encodeToString(serverVersion) )) } } catch (e: IOException) { throw e // Let WorkManager retry } } // 2. Push pending deletes for (note in dao.getDeleting()) { api.deleteNote(note.id) dao.hardDelete(note.id) } // 3. Pull latest from server refresh() } private suspend fun mergeFromServer(remote: NoteDto) { val local = dao.getById(remote.id) when { local == null -> dao.upsert(remote.toEntity(SyncStatus.SYNCED)) local.syncStatus == SyncStatus.SYNCED -> dao.upsert(remote.toEntity(SyncStatus.SYNCED)) remote.updatedAt > local.updatedAt -> dao.upsert(remote.toEntity(SyncStatus.SYNCED)) // else: local is newer and pending — keep it for next push } } // Resolve a conflict: user chooses which version to keep suspend fun resolveConflict(noteId: String, keepLocal: Boolean) { val note = dao.getById(noteId) ?: return if (keepLocal) { dao.upsert(note.copy(conflictServerVersion = null)) syncScheduler.scheduleSync() } else { val serverVersion = Json.decodeFromString<NoteDto>( note.conflictServerVersion!! ) dao.upsert(serverVersion.toEntity(SyncStatus.SYNCED)) } } } // ── SyncWorker ────────────────────────────────────── @HiltWorker class SyncWorker @AssistedInject constructor( @Assisted context: Context, @Assisted params: WorkerParameters, private val repository: NoteRepository ) : CoroutineWorker(context, params) { override suspend fun doWork(): Result { return try { repository.performSync() Result.success() } catch (e: IOException) { if (runAttemptCount < 5) Result.retry() else Result.failure() } } } // ── ViewModel ─────────────────────────────────────── @HiltViewModel class NoteListViewModel @Inject constructor( private val repository: NoteRepository, private val networkMonitor: NetworkMonitor ) : ViewModel() { val notes = repository.observeNotes() .stateIn(viewModelScope, SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), emptyList()) val unsyncedCount = repository.observeUnsyncedCount() .stateIn(viewModelScope, SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), 0) val isOnline = networkMonitor.isOnline .stateIn(viewModelScope, SharingStarted.WhileSubscribed(5000), true) init { viewModelScope.launch { tryRefresh() } } fun refresh() { viewModelScope.launch { tryRefresh() } } private suspend fun tryRefresh() { try { repository.refresh() } catch (_: Exception) {} } }

questions.title: Single Source of Truth
questions.description: In an offline-first architecture, where does the UI read data from?
options: Directly from the REST API via Retrofit, falling back to Room if the network is unavailable
From Room via Flow — the API writes into Room, and Room notifies the UI
From a ViewModel cache that merges API and Room data
From SharedPreferences for small data and the API for large data
questions.explanation: The fundamental principle of offline-first is that Room is the single source of truth. The UI observes Room via Flow and never reads from the API directly. When API data arrives, it's written into Room, which triggers Flow emissions to update the UI. This means the UI works identically whether the device is online or offline — it always reads from Room.
hints: Think about what happens when there's no network — where does data come from?
The UI should show data instantly, not wait for a network call

questions.title: Write Path Ordering
questions.description: What happens when saveNote() is called while the device is in airplane mode?
options: The save fails because there's no network connection
The note is saved to Room with PENDING status immediately; WorkManager waits until the device regains connectivity, then syncs the note to the server
The note is queued in memory and saved to Room only when the network returns
WorkManager throws a NetworkNotAvailableException
questions.explanation: This is the beauty of offline-first. The note is written to Room immediately — the user sees it right away. WorkManager's NetworkType.CONNECTED constraint means the sync job won't execute until the device has a network connection. When connectivity returns (minutes, hours, or days later), WorkManager automatically runs the sync. The user never knows the difference.
hints: Room is a local database — does it need the network?
What does WorkManager do when its constraints aren't met?

questions.title: Worker Routing Problem
questions.description: This SyncWorker bypasses the Repository and calls DAO/API directly. This means conflict resolution logic in the Repository is skipped during background sync. Fix it so the Worker uses the Repository.

checks.message: Worker should depend on Repository, not DAO/API directly
Call the Repository's sync method instead of reimplementing sync logic

questions.explanation: The Worker should call repository.performSync() instead of accessing DAO and API directly. This ensures all data access goes through one path — the Repository — which handles conflict resolution, error handling, and any business logic. If you bypass the Repository, you end up with duplicated logic that drifts out of sync over time.
hints: There should be ONE path to your data, no matter who's accessing it
What class should own all data access logic?

questions.title: Conflict Resolution Strategy
questions.description: A medical records app lets doctors edit patient notes offline from different tablets. Which conflict resolution strategy is most appropriate?
options: Last-write-wins — the most recent timestamp overwrites the other
Server-wins — the server version is always correct
Manual resolution — show both versions and let the doctor choose
CRDTs — auto-merge without user intervention
questions.explanation: For critical data like medical records, manual resolution is the safest strategy. Last-write-wins risks silently discarding a doctor's edits, which could be dangerous. Server-wins would discard offline edits. CRDTs are complex and may not preserve the medical intent of the text. Showing both versions and letting the doctor decide ensures no data is lost and the medical professional makes the judgment call.
hints: What's the cost of silently losing data in a medical context?
Think about which strategies can lose data without the user knowing

questions.title: Cache Invalidation
questions.description: You want to minimize network calls but ensure users see fresh data. Which combination of cache invalidation strategies gives the best balance?
options: TTL only — check every 5 minutes
ETag only — always ask the server if data changed
TTL + ETag + Refresh-on-focus: skip the network if TTL is fresh, use ETag to avoid re-downloading unchanged data, and re-check when the app resumes
FCM push only — let the server tell the client when to refresh
questions.explanation: Combining strategies gives the best results: TTL prevents hammering the server on every screen transition, ETag avoids downloading data that hasn't changed (304 Not Modified is much cheaper than a full response), and Refresh-on-focus catches up after the app has been backgrounded for a while. FCM push is great for real-time updates but shouldn't be your only strategy — push notifications can be delayed or dropped.
hints: Each strategy covers a different weakness of the others
What happens with ETag-only when the user navigates between screens rapidly?

questions.title: Network Monitor with callbackFlow
questions.description: What does this code produce and how does it clean up?
options: A one-shot check that returns true or false once

A Flow that emits true/false whenever connectivity changes, tracking multiple networks (WiFi + cellular), and unregisters the callback when the collector is cancelled — with distinctUntilChanged to suppress duplicate emissions
A Flow that only emits true when WiFi is connected
A hot SharedFlow that keeps emitting even when nobody is collecting
questions.explanation: callbackFlow bridges a callback-based API (NetworkCallback) into a cold Flow. The networks set tracks all active connections — the device is online as long as at least one network exists. awaitClose runs when the collector cancels (e.g., ViewModel cleared), unregistering the callback to prevent leaks. distinctUntilChanged() prevents duplicate true/true emissions when, say, a second WiFi network connects while cellular is already active.
hints: callbackFlow turns callbacks into a Flow — what happens when nobody is collecting?
Why does it track a set of networks instead of just one?

questions.title: UseCase Layer Decision
questions.description: You have a GetNotesUseCase that simply calls repository.observeNotes() with no additional logic. Should you keep it?
options: Yes — Clean Architecture requires a UseCase for every operation
Yes — it will be useful when you add caching logic later
No — it's a pass-through that adds indirection without value. Call the Repository directly until this UseCase needs to coordinate multiple repositories or contain real business logic
No — UseCases should never return Flows
questions.explanation: A UseCase that just delegates to a single Repository method is a pass-through — it adds a file, a class, and a DI binding for zero benefit. The pragmatic approach: start without UseCases and extract one only when a ViewModel method touches 2+ repositories, the same logic appears in 2+ ViewModels, or the UseCase contains real business logic (validation, coordination, transformation). "You might need it later" is not a good reason — YAGNI applies.
hints: What does this class do that the Repository doesn't already do?

questions.title: Soft Delete for Offline Sync
questions.description: This delete function has a problem for offline-first: it hard-deletes the row from Room immediately, so if the device is offline, the server never learns about the deletion. Fix it to use soft-delete with a DELETING status.

checks.message: Mark the note with a DELETING sync status instead of hard-deleting
Schedule a background sync to push the deletion

questions.explanation: In offline-first, never hard-delete locally before confirming with the server. Instead, set the status to DELETING — the DAO query filters these out (WHERE syncStatus != 'DELETING'), so the UI hides them immediately. When the sync worker runs (after connectivity returns), it calls the API to delete server-side, then hard-deletes the local row. This guarantees the deletion propagates even if the device was offline when the user deleted.
hints: What happens if you delete the local row and the network call fails?
How can you hide the note from the UI without actually removing it from Room?
